reflection.theme: The Saturday You Couldn't Have Before
reflection.quote: The future depends on what you do today.
reflection.reading:
Saturdays used to be a problem. Either I was already inside one — somewhere dark by noon, lying to someone by evening — or I was bracing for one, calculating how to make it through without burning anything down. The whole day had a gravity to it. It pulled toward something I couldn't quite name but always ended up doing.

Late May is when that old gravity gets loud again. The light stays longer. Patios fill up. Group chats start moving. There's a version of you the world expects to show up, and that version has been outdated for years, but the invitations don't always know that. What I've learned is that you don't have to argue with any of it. You just have to do the next quiet, ordinary thing — coffee on the porch, a hike before it gets hot, a phone call to someone who actually knows you. That's the Saturday you're building now.

Agency isn't a big dramatic act. It's the small decision, made early in the day, that the day belongs to you. Sobriety didn't shrink my Saturdays. It gave them back. The ski days, the long drives, the mornings I remember — none of that was on the menu when I was drinking. The life you're trying to access is on the other side of a hundred unremarkable Saturdays done sober.
reflection.meditation: What would today look like if it actually belonged to you?

affirmations: I am building a Saturday I don't have to recover from.
Today I get to choose what my day is about, and I'm choosing presence over performance.
I am capable of having fun without erasing myself to get there.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a morning that didn't start in damage control. Grateful for the friends who know the real version of me, the ones who text about hikes and coffee instead of last call. Grateful that an ordinary Saturday in late spring — sunlight, plans I'll actually remember, a body that feels like mine — used to be unimaginable, and is now just Tuesday's cousin.
gratitude.closing: One more day, clear-eyed and mine.
